description: Concurrent use of warfarin and aspirin significantly increases bleeding risk. Both inhibit platelet function and clotting factors. Monitor INR closely; this combination should generally be avoided.
NSAIDs like ibuprofen inhibit platelet aggregation and can cause GI bleeding. Combined with warfarin anticoagulation this creates serious hemorrhage risk.
Naproxen (NSAID) combined with warfarin increases bleeding risk via dual antiplatelet and anticoagulant effect. Avoid concurrent use.
Metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9 significantly increasing warfarin plasma levels and bleeding risk. INR may double. Monitor closely or use alternative antibiotic.
Fluconazole is a strong CYP2C9 inhibitor that dramatically increases warfarin exposure. Can cause serious bleeding. Reduce warfarin dose and monitor INR.
Amiodarone potently inhibits warfarin metabolism leading to markedly elevated INR and life-threatening bleeding. This combination requires extreme caution and INR monitoring.
Rifampin is a powerful CYP450 inducer that drastically reduces warfarin levels potentially leading to thromboembolism. Avoid concurrent use.
Carbamazepine induces CYP2C9 reducing warfarin efficacy. Thrombotic risk increases. Monitor INR and adjust warfarin dose.
Combining SSRIs with MAOIs like phenelzine causes potentially fatal serotonin syndrome: hyperthermia agitation muscle rigidity seizures. Contraindicated. Allow 14-day washout.
SSRI plus MAOI combination is contraindicated due to risk of fatal serotonin syndrome. Allow 14-day washout period between fluoxetine and phenelzine.
SSRIs and MAOIs must not be co-administered due to risk of fatal serotonin syndrome. Contraindicated combination.
Tramadol combined with MAOIs can cause serotonin syndrome and seizures. This combination is contraindicated.
Amiodarone inhibits CYP3A4 increasing simvastatin levels and risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis. Limit simvastatin dose to 20mg/day with amiodarone.
Clarithromycin strongly inhibits CYP3A4 dramatically increasing simvastatin levels causing rhabdomyolysis risk. Contraindicated; hold statin during antibiotic course.
Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 increasing atorvastatin exposure and myopathy risk. Limit atorvastatin to 20mg/day or use alternative antibiotic.
Itraconazole strongly inhibits CYP3A4 causing marked increase in simvastatin levels and high risk of severe myopathy or rhabdomyolysis. Contraindicated.
Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 reducing activation of clopidogrel to its active form. This may reduce antiplatelet efficacy and increase cardiovascular risk.
Amiodarone inhibits P-glycoprotein increasing digoxin levels potentially causing digoxin toxicity: bradycardia heart block nausea. Reduce digoxin dose by 30-50%.
Clarithromycin inhibits P-gp increasing digoxin concentrations and risk of toxicity. Monitor digoxin levels closely.
NSAIDs reduce renal clearance of methotrexate causing toxic accumulation. Can cause severe bone marrow suppression and mucositis. Avoid concurrent use especially with high-dose methotrexate.
Ibuprofen reduces methotrexate renal excretion potentially causing life-threatening toxicity including bone marrow suppression. Avoid concurrent use.
NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance increasing lithium levels and risk of toxicity (tremor confusion arrhythmias). Monitor lithium levels closely.
Naproxen reduces renal lithium clearance. Lithium toxicity risk increases significantly. Monitor serum lithium levels.
Clarithromycin inhibits P-gp and CYP3A4 causing potentially fatal colchicine toxicity including multi-organ failure. Contraindicated especially in renal impairment.
Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 markedly increasing cyclosporine levels and nephrotoxicity risk. Monitor cyclosporine levels and renal function.
Clarithromycin strongly inhibits CYP3A4 increasing tacrolimus levels several-fold. Can cause nephrotoxicity and immunosuppression complications. Monitor levels.
Concurrent use of PDE5 inhibitors with nitrates causes severe potentially fatal hypotension. Absolutely contraindicated.
PDE5 inhibitors combined with nitrates cause severe hypotension. Contraindicated combination.
Amlodipine inhibits CYP3A4 increasing simvastatin exposure and myopathy risk. Limit simvastatin dose to 20mg/day with amlodipine 10mg.
Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 and alters gut flora reducing vitamin K synthesis thereby increasing warfarin effect. Monitor INR during and after ciprofloxacin.
Ciprofloxacin inhibits theophylline metabolism via CYP1A2 causing elevated theophylline levels and toxicity risk (seizures cardiac arrhythmias).
High-dose aspirin displaces valproate from protein binding and inhibits its metabolism increasing free valproate levels and toxicity risk.
Carbamazepine induces valproate metabolism reducing its levels. Valproate also affects carbamazepine metabolite levels. Monitor levels of both drugs.
Valproate inhibits phenytoin metabolism and displaces it from protein binding causing variable and unpredictable phenytoin levels. Monitor closely.
Iodinated contrast media can cause acute kidney injury in patients taking metformin leading to lactic acidosis. Hold metformin 48 hours before and after contrast.

Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase the enzyme responsible for azathioprine metabolism causing severe azathioprine toxicity including bone marrow suppression. Contraindicated or require 75% dose reduction.
Allopurinol blocks mercaptopurine metabolism leading to life-threatening bone marrow suppression and hepatotoxicity. Contraindicated or reduce mercaptopurine dose by 75%.
Dual antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding risk. While used intentionally in ACS treatment the combination requires monitoring for GI and other bleeding.
Combining direct oral anticoagulants with antiplatelet agents increases major bleeding risk. Generally avoid unless benefit clearly outweighs risk.
Anticoagulant combined with antiplatelet agent increases bleeding risk. Monitor for signs of bleeding.
CNS depressant combination (opioid + benzodiazepine) increases risk of profound sedation respiratory depression coma and death. Avoid concurrent use.
Opioids combined with benzodiazepines cause additive CNS and respiratory depression. Can be fatal. Avoid concurrent use or use lowest doses with close monitoring.
Opioid plus benzodiazepine combination is associated with increased risk of respiratory depression and overdose death.
Both drugs prolong QT interval. Concurrent use significantly increases risk of fatal cardiac arrhythmias including torsades de pointes.
Clarithromycin prolongs QT interval and combined with sotalol (which also prolongs QT) greatly increases risk of torsades de pointes.
Both amiodarone and sotalol prolong cardiac repolarization. Combination greatly increases risk of ventricular arrhythmias and torsades de pointes.
